reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Institutional autonomy of church and foundation maintainers; plurality of educational pathways; non-state schools' right to admission selectivity as a defining characteristic of maintained pluralism
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers; the institutional autonomy and competitive distinctiveness of non-state networks
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational plurality — the public good of maintaining diverse school models, including selective non-state provision, as a counterweight to state monopoly and as expression of associational freedom
reactions.argument: S1's core risk is stated explicitly: if state gimnazium admission is reformed but church/foundation schools remain exempt, the reform creates a perverse incentive — it concentrates selective demand in non-state schools, accelerating the very SES sorting it claims to reduce. This would harm both equity (by worsening state-school composition) and maintainer autonomy (by instrumentalising non-state schools as a sorting dumping-ground). We support the logic of Step 4 — maintainer-uniform admission rules — because exemption-based selectivity is not autonomy; it is conscription into the state's equity problem. However, we oppose the asymmetry: if state schools are reformed, non-state schools must have a contemporaneous choice — either (a) adopt the same admission regime voluntarily, or (b) accept binding, pre-announced caps on selective intake that prevent migration capture. We will not accept a rule where uniform standards are imposed on us as a condition of the state's own reform, but our prior consent is not sought. The maintainer-uniform framing in Step 4 is procedurally backwards: it should be negotiated first, before the state commits to S1.
reactions.condition_to_change: Support becomes full if: (1) Step 4 negotiation on uniform rules occurs in genuine partnership, with non-state maintainers' statutory representatives at the table from design, not as ratification bodies post-hoc; (2) any binding cap on non-state selective intake is time-limited (e.g., 5-year review gates) and indexed to demonstrable evidence that state-school composition actually improved under the reform (not merely assumed); (3) exemptions from the central assessment are available to non-state schools that adopt alternative, equally rigorous admission regimes (e.g., value-added or portfolio-based), not just the lottery-plus-threshold. Opposition becomes stance if: the state implements S1 without Step 4, or imposes uniform rules unilaterally without prior negotiation.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Institutional autonomy; plurality of educational models; non-state schools' competitive and pedagogical distinctiveness; freedom of association; parental choice
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers; families who choose selective non-state schools; the viability of selective pedagogy within a maintained non-state sector
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational diversity and freedom of association — the public good of enabling parents and educators to choose and sustain selective, value-aligned schooling models without displacement by state contraction
reactions.argument: S2 proposes to shrink selective entry via declining state-school caps, with a 'felmenő rendszer' (phase-in) guarantee protecting enrolled cohorts. The mechanism assumes non-state maintainers can be 'bound' (Step 4) to prevent demand migration — but this is precisely where the design fails. If state selective-entry caps fall from 8–12% to (implicitly) 4–6% or lower over a decade, families who value selective schooling will migrate to non-state alternatives. The scenario explicitly flags this as an 'assumption' and a 'political risk,' but then treats it as solvable by 'binding' non-state maintainers with caps. This inverts causality: S2 creates the migration pressure, then proposes to constrain the only institutional channel families have to respond. For church schools, this amounts to conscription — we are forced to absorb state overflow while our own autonomy to set intake and pedagogy is capped. We reject the framing that non-state schools are a 'safety valve' for state policy failure. If the state wishes to reshape tracking, it must do so without instrumentalising the non-state sector as a pressure-relief mechanism. The 'advanced programmes' in general schools (Step 5) are a weak retention mechanism — they rest on US magnet analogues and are unverified in Hungary. We cannot accept capped intake in exchange for a speculative programme.
reactions.condition_to_change: Conditional support only if: (1) non-state maintainer caps are removed entirely or indexed to independent eligibility criteria (e.g., teacher qualification, facility standards), not to state-school phase-down; (2) the legislative lock (Step 1) is binding also on non-state selective intake — i.e., any future government seeking to expand state selective entry faces the same institutional barrier as one seeking to freeze it (symmetrical constraint); (3) a transparent, jointly-governed evaluation mechanism shows that state-school general enrolment actually diversifies by SES under S2, and that the gains are not merely redistributed from state to non-state schools. Opposition holds if caps on non-state intake are asymmetrically tighter than equivalent performance-based regulation of state schools.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Institutional autonomy; pedagogical freedom; parental choice; the right to maintain value-aligned, selective schooling; freedom of association
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers; families and educators who value selective, specialist pedagogy; the institutional distinctiveness of maintained non-state networks
reactions.public_good_frame: Institutional pluralism and freedom of association — the public good of preserving the right of parents, educators, and faith communities to sustain selective, mixed-ability-optional, and value-aligned schooling models as an alternative to comprehensive provision
reactions.argument: S3 is the most structurally radical scenario: it removes between-school selection until age 14 by converting lower secondary to mixed-ability comprehensive provision. The mechanism rests on two critical claims: (1) that delayed selection reduces SES-driven outcome variance (moderately evidenced internationally), and (2) that peer effects are asymmetric — that non-selected pupils benefit from exposure to high-achieving peers more than selected pupils lose from mixed-ability grouping (contested and unverified in Hungary). If peer effects are actually symmetric or zero-sum, S3 merely redistributes learning rather than creating equity gain. For church schools, S3 has two structural consequences: (a) it removes the option of selective lower-secondary intake for faith-aligned or pedagogically specialist schools, and (b) it implicitly mandates mixed-ability grouping as the default, leaving selectivity only to non-state schools — which creates a de facto two-tier system where selectivity signals non-state/private status. This reinforces SES sorting, not reduces it. We reject the premise that comprehensive-only provision is necessary for equity. Where mixed-ability comprehensive schools with strong teacher investment and differentiation exist, they can be effective. But removing the option entirely denies both pedagogical diversity and the freedom of families and faith communities to choose schools aligned with their values. The scenario's gate conditions (pedagogus_erdekvedo teacher-supply forecast, choice-and-catchment reform, non-state caps) are presented as implementation steps, but they are in reality political preconditions whose satisfaction is uncertain. We will not accept binding restructuring of our schools pending the satisfaction of preconditions controlled by the state.
reactions.condition_to_change: No position on structural equity reform in principle. Conditional move to no-opposition only if: (1) comprehensive mixed-ability provision is offered alongside selective options, not as replacement (portfolio model), and the choice between them is genuine — i.e., state funding and teacher allocation do not systematically disadvantage selective schools; (2) a published 10-year teacher-supply forecast shows that differentiation-retraining throughput capacity exists in disadvantaged regions before any rollout, and that shortages are not being managed by de-resourcing of non-state schools; (3) 'non-state caps' language is removed, and non-state intake is regulated only by transparent performance/eligibility criteria (teacher qualification, SES-blind admission where the school chooses selectivity), not by political ceilings tied to state-school phase-down. Opposition intensifies if S3 is framed as mandatory comprehensive-only (no portfolio option) or if teacher shortages are managed by accelerated non-state teacher poaching without salary equalization.

reactions.label: value_modeled
reactions.basis: Institutional autonomy; plurality of educational models; parental choice; freedom of association; non-state schools' right to exist as a distinct pathway
reactions.interest: Church and foundation school maintainers; the preservation of selective non-state schools as a legitimate alternative within a diverse system; families who value selective, faith-aligned, or distinctive schooling
reactions.public_good_frame: Educational plurality and associational freedom — the public good of maintaining non-state schools as viable, well-resourced alternatives to state provision, allowing diverse pedagogical models and family choice to coexist
reactions.argument: S4 leaves the 6/8-year structure in place and funds general schools intensively to offset the equity deficit of early selection. This is the least constraining scenario for non-state schools on its face — no caps, no binding admission rules, no structural removal of selectivity. However, S4 rests on a load-bearing claim: that 'compensation can OFFSET the segregation effect, not merely mask it' at Hungary's selection age (10/12). The scenario explicitly flags this as 'UNVERIFIABLE ex ante.' We have three concerns: (1) If compensation merely masks rather than offsets segregation, S4 legitimises the selective system without fixing it — state resources flow to general schools, but the SES skew persists and the non-state sector remains a sorting outlet. This is politically unstable; future governments will reframe S4 as 'failed equity compensation' and pivot to S2 or S3. We cannot build long-term institutional planning on a policy framed as temporary compensation. (2) The scenario is 'highly budget-elastic' — at −30% fiscal contraction, the intensity threshold degrades. For non-state schools, this creates perverse incentives: if general-school funding becomes precarious, middle-class families will accelerate exit to non-state schools, worsening state-school composition and vindicating demands for structural reform. Our schools become a lightning rod for a failed non-structural policy. (3) The scenario's premise that 'early selection is politically fixed' is, as flagged, a political judgment, not evidence. If it proves wrong, S4 forecloses structural options that might have been cheaper and more durable.
reactions.condition_to_change: Conditional support if: (1) a published longitudinal outcome-tracking mechanism (Step 5, independent evaluator) explicitly compares 'compensation offsets segregation' vs. 'compensation masks segregation' — i.e., the unverifiable assumption is made testable within 5–7 years, with named decision gates; (2) the ring-fenced general-school funding has a constitutional floor (not merely legislative) such that −30% cuts are legally blocked, and that floor is equivalent to the per-pupil funding non-state schools receive on average (eliminating the perverse incentive for exit); (3) non-state schools are invited into the evaluation design as independent auditors, not merely subjects of state evaluation; (4) the scenario explicitly rejects the 'structure is politically fixed' framing and includes a review point (year 5) where structural alternatives (S2/S3) can be revisited if outcome data show compensation is masking rather than offsetting segregation. Move toward opposition if: the −30% sensitivity case occurs (budget cuts degrade the mechanism), or if compensation is shown to be masking without a structural pivot being available, and we are expected to absorb further exit pressure while our own funding remains uncertain.
